step_1_context.text: Some verbs can be followed by an -ing form or a to-infinitive with little difference in meaning. These include: attempt, begin, can't bear, cease, continue, hate, intend, like, love, prefer, start.

step_2_explanation.simple_rule: Some verbs can be followed by an -ing form or a to-infinitive with little difference in meaning.
step_2_explanation.formula: Other verbs can be followed by an -ing form or a to-infinitive, but there is a difference in meaning.
step_2_explanation.common_mistake: Pay attention to the context when choosing the correct form.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 32.1.1 I'm beginning ___ that it was a mistake to come here.
32.1.2 I like ___ the room every morning before I start work.
32.2.1 I regret ___ him that he hadn't got the job.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Regret + to-infinitive is used for bad news/formal statements.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 32.2.2 I regret ___ him that he hadn't got the job.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Regret + -ing is for feeling sorry about past actions.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 32.2.3 After two years in the job, she went on ___ the company's youngest manager.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Go on + to-infinitive = move to a new action.
